mnemonics: Think of a fork in the road: SOFT forks are like adding a new HOV lane (backwards compatible, old cars can still drive), while HARD forks are like building a new toll road (not backwards compatible, requires everyone to upgrade their car to use it).
keyPoints: A fork is a change in a blockchain's protocol, creating two separate versions of the chain.
A soft fork is a backwards-compatible update; old nodes still accept new blocks.
A hard fork is a non-backwards-compatible update, resulting in a permanent split from the original chain.
commonMistakes: Believing a hard fork always creates a new cryptocurrency; it only does if there is a permanent split and the new chain is adopted.
Thinking soft forks require all nodes to upgrade; only miners need to upgrade to enforce new rules, while old nodes can still participate.
Assuming forks are always contentious; they can also be planned, agreed-upon upgrades.
